role_summary: President of the Brotherhood of Sleeping Car Porters (1925-1968), the first Black-led labor union to win a collective bargaining agreement; principal organizer of the 1941 March on Washington Movement that compelled FDR's Executive Order 8802 desegregating defense industries; principal organizer of the 1963 March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom.

photo.photographer: John Bottega (New York World-Telegram & Sun staff photographer)
photo.repository: Wikimedia Commons (via Library of Congress, New York World-Telegram & Sun collection)
photo.license: Public domain (Library of Congress: no known copyright restrictions; NYWTS collection donated to LoC)

ai_reading: Seven corpus voices discuss Randolph: Timothy Jenkins (entry 120, SNCC), Clifford D. Browner Jr. (entry 23), Junius Williams (entry 72, Newark Black Power), Courtland Cox (entry 24, MOW transportation logistics under Bayard Rustin, Randolph's deputy), H. Jack Geiger (entry 54), Cleveland Sellers (entry 22), and Rick Tuttle (entry 105). The cluster crosses labor-and-March-on-Washington organizing (Cox is the most directly aligned, having coordinated MOW logistics that Randolph and Rustin oversaw) with broader SNCC and Black Power organizing. The embedding therefore reads Randolph's vocabulary as integrating labor and civil rights traditions the cultural reception often treats as separate.
biographical_paragraph: A. Philip Randolph is discussed in this corpus by 7 interviewees across the catalog (Timothy Jenkins (entry 120), Clifford D. Browner Jr. (entry 23), Junius Williams (entry 72), Courtland Cox (entry 24), H. Jack Geiger (partial read) (entry 54), Dr. Cleveland Sellers (entry 22), Rick Tuttle (entry 105)), but was not interviewed for the Civil Rights History Project; this catalog page treats A. Philip Randolph as an external figure whose presence in the archive is mediated through other voices. The canonical scholarly source the catalog cites for biographical orientation appears as [src: 1] below.

interview_snippets.speaker: Courtland Cox
interview_snippets.lead_in: SNCC program coordinator Courtland Cox recounts the dispute over John Lewis's draft speech at the 1963 March on Washington, when Randolph appealed to the SNCC delegation to revise it:
interview_snippets.quote: A. Philip Randolph said, I know, you know, you know, he gave us our propers, but he said, look, I have worked since 1941 to make this happen. And it is important that the coalition is held together. And it is out of respect for A. Philip Randolph that we were, you know, we went in the back of the Lincoln Memorial,

interview_snippets.speaker: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
interview_snippets.lead_in: SNCC program director Cleveland Sellers describes the same March on Washington moment, when Randolph came personally to the SNCC organizers about John Lewis's speech:
interview_snippets.quote: A. Philip Randolph actually came to the SNCCers and said, you know, I've been working on this ever since 1940. This is, and I'm close to having a dream fulfill. Can you help me with this?

interview_snippets.speaker: H. Jack Geiger
interview_snippets.lead_in: Civil rights physician H. Jack Geiger recalls being recruited as a University of Wisconsin freshman in early 1942 to organize on campus for Randolph's first March on Washington Movement:
interview_snippets.quote: And he must have got my name from Canada somehow saying they were trying to organize on campus, on college campuses everywhere for this much overlooked first threatened March on Washington that A. A. Philip Randolph and Bayard Rustin were organizing in protest against discrimination and defense plans.

interview_snippets.speaker: Timothy Jenkins
interview_snippets.lead_in: National Student Association vice president Timothy Jenkins places Ella Baker within Randolph's orbit, tracing her early work to the Brotherhood of Sleeping Car Porters:
interview_snippets.quote: She had been initially associated with the Brotherhood of Sleeping Car Porters and was a confidante and friend of a Philip Randolph and had supported his call early on for March on Washington back in the 40s and 50s.

interview_snippets.speaker: Mildred Bond Roxborough
interview_snippets.lead_in: Longtime NAACP staff member Mildred Bond Roxborough describes the coalition behind President Roosevelt's wartime fair-employment order, with Randolph working alongside Walter White and Eleanor Roosevelt:
interview_snippets.quote: it was possible to get Mrs. Roosevelt to work with Walter White and Philip Randolph and get the President, then Roosevelt, to understand that an executive order, to desegregating federal installations, should be issued.

sources.title: Jervis Anderson, A. Philip Randolph: A Biographical Portrait
